did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP), a machine learning technique, for multiple feature construction and classification of skin cancer images (melanoma detection and multi-class classification) from datasets like PH2 and Dermofit.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing a novel classification approach using multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) for skin cancer image classification. It details specific methods (feature extraction, GP parameters), datasets used (PH2, Dermofit), and reports new results (accuracy metrics, statistical significance tests) comparing the proposed method against baselines, which aligns with the definition of a research paper producing new results.

title: Genetic Programming for Multiple Feature Construction in Skin Cancer Image Classification
brief_summary: This study develops a novel skin cancer classification approach using multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) to construct multiple features from lesion images for both melanoma detection and multi-class classification. The method employs wavelet-based texture features from multiple color channels alongside local binary patterns, lesion color variation, and geometrical border shape features. The proposed WGP-5 method was evaluated on the PH2 and Dermofit datasets using 10-fold cross-validation and compared against single-tree GP, existing multi-tree GP, and six non-GP classification algorithms. Results indicate that the proposed method significantly outperformed all baseline methods, achieving accuracies of 89.77% and 97.26% for binary classification on the PH2 and Dermofit datasets, respectively. The study demonstrates that constructing informative features via MTGP effectively handles both binary and complex multi-class skin cancer image classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the malignant class in binary classification on PH2 and Dermofit datasets; also part of the 10-class multi-class classification task on Dermofit.
Used as the benign class in binary classification on PH2 and Dermofit datasets; also part of the 10-class multi-class classification task on Dermofit.

paper_terms: ten different classes of skin cancer
lesion_types_studied.details: The Dermofit dataset contains 10 classes of skin lesions. The paper notes that the PH2 dataset has 3 classes and Dermofit has 10 classes. Specific names of the other 8 classes in Dermofit are not listed in the provided text, though reference [18] mentions 'non-melanoma skin lesions'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification (Melanocytic Nevus vs. Malignant Melanoma) and Multi-class classification (10 classes of skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between benign (Melanocytic Nevus) and malignant (Malignant Melanoma) lesions, and multi-class classification to distinguish between ten different classes of skin cancer. The primary task is classification, as evidenced by the use of accuracy metrics, confusion matrices, and statistical significance tests (Wilcoxon signed-rank test) on class labels.

datasets_used.name: PH2 dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 200 dermoscopy images with a size of roughly 768 × 560 pixels, captured using a specialized dermatoscope device. It contains three classes: melanoma, common nevus, and atypical nevus.

use.training: Used for binary classification (40 melanoma vs. 160 benign) and multi-class classification (3 classes) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for binary classification (40 melanoma vs. 160 benign) and multi-class classification (3 classes) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for binary classification (40 melanoma vs. 160 benign) and multi-class classification (3 classes) via 10-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: Dermofit Image Library
datasets_used.description: A dataset consisting of 1300 lesion images taken from a standard camera under standardized conditions. The lesions belong to ten classes, and each image is provided with a gold standard diagnosis.
classes_used: Eight other non-melanoma skin lesion classes (specific names not listed in text)

use.training: Used for binary classification (Melanocytic Nevus vs. Malignant Melanoma) and multi-class classification (10 classes) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for binary classification (Melanocytic Nevus vs. Malignant Melanoma) and multi-class classification (10 classes) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for binary classification (Melanocytic Nevus vs. Malignant Melanoma) and multi-class classification (10 classes) via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To identify the significance of the proposed method's performance compared to baseline methods and check which method has better ability to correctly classify lesion images.
Used as the threshold for the Wilcoxon signed-rank test to determine statistical significance.
To report the results of the 30 GP runs and the deterministic methods across 10-fold cross-validation.
To split the data into training and testing sets for evaluating the performance of the classification algorithms.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the ability to correctly classify lesion images between the proposed WGP-5 method and the baseline methods.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the ability to correctly classify lesion images between the proposed WGP-5 method and the baseline methods (specifically, that WGP-5 performs significantly better).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The test was used to identify the significance of the proposed method's performance compared to baseline methods. Symbols '+' and '-' were used to represent that the proposed method significantly outperforms or is significantly worse than the corresponding method, respectively, while '=' indicates similar performance. The results showed that WGP-5 significantly outperformed all baseline methods.

PH2 dataset.notes: WGP-5 significantly outperformed all baseline methods in binary classification. In multi-class classification, WGP-5 with J48 achieved the highest performance among the proposed methods. The PH2 dataset contains 3 classes (Melanoma, Common Nevus, Atypical Nevus).

Dermofit Image Library.notes: WGP-5 significantly outperformed all baseline methods in binary classification. In multi-class classification (10 classes), performance dropped significantly for all methods compared to binary classification. WGP-5 with J48 achieved 50.08 ± 3.27% accuracy. The Dermofit dataset contains 10 classes.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-tree Genetic Programming (MTGP) for feature construction combined with standard classification algorithms (Naive Bayes, SVM, k-NN, J48 Decision Trees, Random Forest, MLP)
architecture_used.input_size: PH2 dataset: 768 × 560 pixels; Dermofit dataset: standard camera images (specific dimensions not reported)

architecture_details.feature_construction_method: Multi-tree Genetic Programming (MTGP) with 5 trees (Constructed Features - CFs)
terminal_set_features: Wavelet-based texture features (416 features from RGB and luminance channels using 3-level pyramid-structured wavelet decomposition)
Local Binary Patterns (LBP) from RGB channels (177 features)
Local Binary Patterns (LBP) from gray images (59 features)

training_details.fitness_function: Accuracy of the wrapper classification algorithm

classification_or_output_head.mlp_parameters: Momentum, learning rate, training epochs, and number of units in single hidden layer adopted from previous methods [7], [20] (specific values not reported)

preprocessing_techniques: The paper did not report applying preprocessing techniques before feature extraction; it explicitly states in the future work section that they would investigate the impact of using pre-processing techniques to remove artifacts such as hair and reflection.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report using any data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, synthetic data generation, or GANs). The study explicitly states in the future work section that they would investigate the impact of using pre-processing techniques to remove artifacts such as hair and reflection, implying these were not applied during the reported experiments.

conclusions: The proposed multi-tree genetic programming (MTGP) method, specifically WGP-5, significantly outperformed all baseline methods (including existing multi-tree GP, single-tree GP, and six non-GP classification algorithms) in both binary and multi-class skin cancer image classification tasks.
The WGP-5 method achieved the highest accuracy for binary classification on the PH2 dataset (89.77 ± 1.84% with Naive Bayes) and the Dermofit Image Library (97.26 ± 1.25% with SVM).

In multi-class classification, the WGP-5 method with J48 achieved the highest performance among the proposed methods, reaching 85.82 ± 1.60% on the PH2 dataset and 74.05 ± 1.52% on the Dermofit dataset.

Constructing informative features via MTGP effectively handles both binary and complex multi-class skin cancer image classification tasks, demonstrating potential to assist dermatologists in real-time diagnosis.

The use of wavelet-based texture features from multiple color channels, combined with other features via MTGP, significantly improves the distinguishing ability of classifiers compared to existing approaches that fail to effectively combine various features.

While wavelet features helped non-GP and single-tree GP methods achieve performance gains in binary classification, they remained unable to cope well with the difficult multi-class classification task compared to the proposed WGP-5 method.

The proposed method demonstrates robustness across multiple image modalities (dermoscopy images from PH2 and standard camera images from Dermofit), addressing limitations of existing approaches developed for only single image modality.
The study acknowledges that it did not apply preprocessing techniques to remove artifacts such as hair and reflection, identifying this as an area for future work.
important_remarks: The study did not apply preprocessing techniques to remove artifacts such as hair and reflection, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation and area for future work.

Performance on the Dermofit dataset (standard camera images) was highly dependent on the classifier; while SVM achieved 97.26% accuracy in binary classification, other classifiers like k-NN performed significantly worse (86.04%), indicating sensitivity to classifier choice for non-dermoscopy images.

Multi-class classification performance dropped significantly compared to binary classification (e.g., WGP-5 with J48 dropped from 96.99% to 74.05% on Dermofit), highlighting the difficulty of distinguishing among 10 lesion classes.

The PH2 dataset is small (200 images) and the Dermofit dataset, while larger (1300 images), consists of images taken from a standard camera under standardized conditions, which may limit generalizability to diverse clinical settings with varying image quality.

The paper does not report an independent external validation set; all results are derived from 10-fold cross-validation on the two specific datasets, which may lead to optimistic performance estimates.

The proposed WGP-5 method significantly outperformed baselines in binary classification but showed mixed results in multi-class classification, with some baseline methods (like Wavelet features in WGP-1) performing surprisingly well on binary tasks but poorly on multi-class tasks, suggesting potential overfitting or feature redundancy in simpler tasks.
